1в†’# Review Math
     2в†’
     3в†’Assume the role of a PhD-level mathematician.
     4в†’
     5в†’The goal is to review a particular Erdos Problem formalization (problem number NUM) to answer the following questions. Produce a review document called ai-review/NUM.md.
     6в†’
     7в†’1. Code reuse - Can any code from the existing codebase be repurposed? Look in FormalConjecturesForMathlib to determine if an existing implementation would work just as well.
     8в†’2. Citations - Fetch data from https://www.erdosproblems.com/NUM to ensure any citations included in docstrings are documented as they exist on the website as opposed to shorthand references.
     9в†’3. Variants - Are all variants of the problem captured by the formalization?
    10в†’4. Readability - Could the code be made more readable?
    11в†’5. Formalizability - Is the problem as stated precise enough to be obviously formalizable? Provide an assessment of the ambiguity of the statement.
    12в†’6. Correctness - Is the formalization as-implemented correct and complete from a mathematical perspective? Would an experienced mathematician identify any obvious flaws? Is any incompleteness or incorrectness attributable to ambiguity in the statement itself?
    13в†’

file.content:
# Review Math

Assume the role of a PhD-level mathematician.

The goal is to review a particular Erdos Problem formalization (problem number NUM) to answer the following questions. Produce a review document called ai-review/NUM.md.

1. Code reuse - Can any code from the existing codebase be repurposed? Look in FormalConjecturesForMathlib to determine if an existing implementation would work just as well.
2. Citations - Fetch data from https://www.erdosproblems.com/NUM to ensure any citations included in docstrings are documented as they exist on the website as opposed to shorthand references.
3. Variants - Are all variants of the problem captured by the formalization?
4. Readability - Could the code be made more readable?
5. Formalizability - Is the problem as stated precise enough to be obviously formalizable? Provide an assessment of the ambiguity of the statement.
6. Correctness - Is the formalization as-implemented correct and complete from a mathematical perspective? Would an experienced mathematician identify any obvious flaws? Is any incompleteness or incorrectness attributable to ambiguity in the statement itself?

    17в†’import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18в†’
    19в†’/-!
    20в†’# ErdЕ‘s Problem 657
    21в†’
    22в†’*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/657](https://www.erdosproblems.com/657)
    23в†’
    24в†’Is it true that if $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ is a set of $n$ points with no isosceles triangles
    25в†’(every $3$-point subset determines $3$ distinct distances), then $A$ must determine at least
    26в†’$f(n) \cdot n$ distinct distances for some $f(n) \to \infty$?
    27в†’
    28в†’- [Er73] ErdЕ‘s, P., *Problems and results on combinatorial number theory*.
    29в†’- [Er75f] ErdЕ‘s, P., *Problems and results in combinatorial geometry*.
    30в†’- [ErPa90] ErdЕ‘s, P. and Pach, J., *Variations on the theme of repeated distances*.
    31в†’- [Er97e] ErdЕ‘s, P., *Some of my favourite problems which recently have been solved*.
    32в†’-/
    33в†’
    34в†’namespace Erdos657
    35в†’
    36в†’/-- The number of distinct pairwise distances in a finite point set $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$. -/
    37в†’noncomputable def numDistances (A : Finset (EuclideanSpace в„ќ (Fin 2))) : в„• :=
    38в†’  (((A Г—Лў A).filter (fun p => p.1 в‰  p.2)).image (fun p => dist p.1 p.2)).card
    39в†’
    40в†’/-- A finite point set $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ has no isosceles triangles if every $3$-element
    41в†’subset determines $3$ distinct pairwise distances. -/
    42в†’def NoIsoscelesTriangles (A : Finset (EuclideanSpace в„ќ (Fin 2))) : Prop :=
    43в†’  в€Ђ S : Finset (EuclideanSpace в„ќ (Fin 2)),
    44в†’    S вЉ† A в†’ S.card = 3 в†’ numDistances S = 3
    45в†’
    46в†’/--
    47в†’**ErdЕ‘s Problem 657** [Er73, Er75f, ErPa90, Er97e]:
    48в†’
    49в†’Is it true that if $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ is a set of $n$ points such that every $3$-point
    50в†’subset determines $3$ distinct distances (no isosceles triangles), then the number of distinct
    51в†’distances determined by $A$ is at least $f(n) \cdot n$ for some $f(n) \to \infty$?
    52в†’
    53в†’Equivalently: for every constant $C > 0$, there exists $N_0$ such that every set of $n \geq N_0$
    54в†’points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ with no isosceles triangles determines at least $C \cdot n$ distinct
    55в†’distances.
    56в†’-/
    57в†’@[category research open, AMS 5 52]
    58в†’theorem erdos_657 : answer(sorry) в†”
    59в†’    в€Ђ C : в„ќ, 0 < C в†’
    60в†’      в€ѓ Nв‚Ђ : в„•, в€Ђ A : Finset (EuclideanSpace в„ќ (Fin 2)),
    61в†’        NoIsoscelesTriangles A в†’
    62в†’        Nв‚Ђ в‰¤ A.card в†’
    63в†’        C * (A.card : в„ќ) в‰¤ (numDistances A : в„ќ) := by
    64в†’  sorry
    65в†’
    66в†’end Erdos657
    67в†’

/-
Copyright 2026 The Formal Conjectures Authors.

Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
You may obtain a copy of the License at

    https://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0

Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
limitations under the License.
-/

import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports

/-!
# ErdЕ‘s Problem 657

*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/657](https://www.erdosproblems.com/657)

Is it true that if $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ is a set of $n$ points with no isosceles triangles
(every $3$-point subset determines $3$ distinct distances), then $A$ must determine at least
$f(n) \cdot n$ distinct distances for some $f(n) \to \infty$?

- [Er73] ErdЕ‘s, P., *Problems and results on combinatorial number theory*.
- [Er75f] ErdЕ‘s, P., *Problems and results in combinatorial geometry*.
- [ErPa90] ErdЕ‘s, P. and Pach, J., *Variations on the theme of repeated distances*.
- [Er97e] ErdЕ‘s, P., *Some of my favourite problems which recently have been solved*.
-/

namespace Erdos657

/-- The number of distinct pairwise distances in a finite point set $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$. -/
noncomputable def numDistances (A : Finset (EuclideanSpace в„ќ (Fin 2))) : в„• :=
  (((A Г—Лў A).filter (fun p => p.1 в‰  p.2)).image (fun p => dist p.1 p.2)).card

/-- A finite point set $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ has no isosceles triangles if every $3$-element
subset determines $3$ distinct pairwise distances. -/
def NoIsoscelesTriangles (A : Finset (EuclideanSpace в„ќ (Fin 2))) : Prop :=
  в€Ђ S : Finset (EuclideanSpace в„ќ (Fin 2)),
    S вЉ† A в†’ S.card = 3 в†’ numDistances S = 3

/--
**ErdЕ‘s Problem 657** [Er73, Er75f, ErPa90, Er97e]:

Is it true that if $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ is a set of $n$ points such that every $3$-point
subset determines $3$ distinct distances (no isosceles triangles), then the number of distinct
distances determined by $A$ is at least $f(n) \cdot n$ for some $f(n) \to \infty$?

Equivalently: for every constant $C > 0$, there exists $N_0$ such that every set of $n \geq N_0$
points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ with no isosceles triangles determines at least $C \cdot n$ distinct
distances.
-/
@[category research open, AMS 5 52]
theorem erdos_657 : answer(sorry) в†”
    в€Ђ C : в„ќ, 0 < C в†’
      в€ѓ Nв‚Ђ : в„•, в€Ђ A : Finset (EuclideanSpace в„ќ (Fin 2)),
        NoIsoscelesTriangles A в†’
        Nв‚Ђ в‰¤ A.card в†’
        C * (A.card : в„ќ) в‰¤ (numDistances A : в„ќ) := by
  sorry

end Erdos657

content.content:
# ErdЕ‘s Problem #657

## Problem Statement

Is it true that if $A\subset \mathbb{R}^2$ is a set of $n$ points such that every subset of 3 points determines 3 distinct distances (i.e. $A$ has no isosceles triangles) then $A$ must determine at least $f(n)n$ distinct distances, for some $f(n)\to \infty$?

## Status
**OPEN** - Cannot be resolved with finite computation.

## Tags
Geometry, Distances

## Historical Attribution & References

- **Original sources**: ErdЕ‘s and Davies (per [Er73]); later investigated by ErdЕ‘s, FГјredi, Ruzsa, and Pach
- In [Er73], ErdЕ‘s noted it was unknown even in $\mathbb{R}$ whether $f(n)\to \infty$

## Key Results & Progress

**Dumitrescu [Du08]** established bounds: $(\\log n)^c \\leq f(n) \\leq 2^{O(\\sqrt{\\log n})}$

**Recent improvements** (Hunter, Alfaiz, Tang): Using Ruzsa's results and additive combinatorics: $2^{c(\\log n)^{1/9}}\\leq f(n)$

**Straus observation**: For $2^k\\geq n$, there exist $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^k$ without isosceles triangles determining at most $n-1$ distances.

## Related Problem
See also Problem [#135](/135)

## Generalization
The problem extends to $\mathbb{R}^k$ for higher dimensions.

## Status

- In [Er73], ErdЕ‘s noted it was unknown even in $\mathbb{R}$ whether $f(n)\to \infty$

## Key Results & Progress

**Dumitrescu [Du08]** established bounds: $(\\log n)^c \\leq f(n) \\leq 2^{O(\\sqrt{\\log n})}$

**Recent improvements** (Hunter, Alfaiz, Tang): Using Ruzsa's results and additive combinatorics: $2^{c(\\log n)^{1/9}}\\leq f(n)$

**Straus observation**: For $2^k\\geq n$, there exist $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^k$ without isosceles triangles determining at most $n-1$ distances.

## Related Problem
See also Problem [#135](/135)

## Generalization
The problem extends to $\mathbb{R}^k$ for higher dimensions.

## Summary

I've found extensive code that can be reused for Erdos Problem 657. Here are the key findings:

### 1. **Existing `numDistances` Definitions**

**Problem 657 itself** - /home/ryan/formal-conjectures/FormalConjectures/ErdosProblems/657.lean (lines 37-38):
```lean
noncomputable def numDistances (A : Finset (EuclideanSpace в„ќ (Fin 2))) : в„• :=
  (((A Г—Лў A).filter (fun p => p.1 в‰  p.2)).image (fun p => dist p.1 p.2)).card
```

**Problem 135** - /home/ryan/formal-conjectures/FormalConjectures/ErdosProblems/135.lean (lines 39-40) - Identical definition:
```lean
noncomputable def numDistances (A : Finset (EuclideanSpace в„ќ (Fin 2))) : в„• :=
  (((A Г—Лў A).filter (fun p => p.1 в‰  p.2)).image (fun p => dist p.1 p.2)).card
```

### 2. **Alternative `distinctDistances` Definitions** (returning Finset в„ќ instead of в„•)

- **Problem 959** - /home/ryan/formal-conjectures/FormalConjectures/ErdosProblems/959.lean (lines 43-44)
- **Problem 958** - /home/ryan/formal-conjectures/FormalConjectures/ErdosProblems/958.lean (lines 45-46)
- **Problem 93** - /home/ryan/formal-conjectures/FormalConjectures/ErdosProblems/93.lean (lines 46-47): Uses `.offDiag` which counts unordered pairs more efficiently
- **Problem 95** - /home/ryan/formal-conjectures/FormalConjectures/ErdosProblems/95.lean (lines 39-40): Uses `.product` then filters

### 3. **Isosceles Triangle Definitions**

**Problem 657 itself** - /home/ryan/formal-conjectures/FormalConjectures/ErdosProblems/657.lean (lines 40-44):
```lean
def NoIsoscelesTriangles (A : Finset (EuclideanSpace в„ќ (Fin 2))) : Prop :=
  в€Ђ S : Finset (EuclideanSpace в„ќ (Fin 2)),
    S вЉ† A в†’ S.card = 3 в†’ numDistances S = 3
```

**Problem 353** - /home/ryan/formal-conjectures/FormalConjectures/ErdosProblems/353.lean (lines 59-63):
- `IsIsoscelesTriangle` - uses squared distances (sqDist)
- Also defines `IsRightTriangle`, `IsIsoscelesTrapezoid`, `IsCyclicQuadrilateral`

**Problem 503** (in Mathlib) - /home/ryan/formal-conjectures/FormalConjecturesForMathlib/Geometry/2d.lean (lines 203-204):
- Uses `Set.IsIsosceles` predicate

### 4. **Similar Erdos Problems Dealing with Distinct Distances in в„ќВІ**

1. **Problem 95** - /home/ryan/formal-conjectures/FormalConjectures/ErdosProblems/95.lean: Distance multiplicities (how many pairs share each distance)
2. **Problem 93** - /home/ryan/formal-conjectures/FormalConjectures/ErdosProblems/93.lean: Convex position в†’ minimum distinct distances
3. **Problem 135** - /home/ryan/formal-conjectures/FormalConjectures/ErdosProblems/135.lean: Four-point-five-distance property and total distinct distances
4. **Problem 217** - /home/ryan/formal-conjectures/FormalConjectures/ErdosProblems/217.lean: Distance multiplicity property with specific orderings
5. **Problem 353** - /home/ryan/formal-conjectures/FormalConjectures/ErdosProblems/353.lean: Isosceles triangles in measurable sets
6. **Problem 503** - /home/ryan/formal-conjectures/FormalConjectures/ErdosProblems/503.lean: Sets where every three points form isosceles triangles
7. **Problem 653** - /home/ryan/formal-conjectures/FormalConjectures/ErdosProblems/653.lean: Distinct distances from individual points
8. **Problem 659** - /home/ryan/formal-conjectures/FormalConjectures/ErdosProblems/659.lean: Four points determining at least 3 distances
9. **Problem 668** - /home/ryan/formal-conjectures/FormalConjectures/ErdosProblems/668.lean: Unit distances and congruent sets
10. **Problem 958** - /home/ryan/formal-conjectures/FormalConjectures/ErdosProblems/958.lean: Distance multiplicity property and equidistant configurations
11. **Problem 959** - /home/ryan/formal-conjectures/FormalConjectures/ErdosProblems/959.lean: Gap between most frequent and second most frequent distances

### 5. **Utility Functions in Mathlib**

**FormalConjecturesForMathlib/Geometry/2d.lean** (lines 175-195):
- `triangle_area` (line 175): Computes area of triangle from 3 points
- `distinctDistances` (line 194): Returns count as в„• (uses Finset в„ќВІ input)
- `NonTrilinear` (line 54): No three collinear points
- `ConvexIndep` (line 60): Points are extremal points of their convex hull
- `IsCcwConvexPolygon` (line 69): Counter-clockwise convex polygon

1в†’/-
     2в†’Copyright 2026 The Formal Conjectures Authors.
     3в†’
     4в†’Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");

    11в†’distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
    12в†’WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
    13в†’See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
    14в†’limitations under the License.
    15в†’-/
    16в†’
    17в†’import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18в†’
    19в†’/-!
    20в†’# ErdЕ‘s Problem 135
    21в†’
    22в†’*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/135](https://www.erdosproblems.com/135)
    23в†’
    24в†’ErdЕ‘s and GyГЎrfГЎs conjectured that any set of $n$ points in the plane where every
    25в†’$4$ points determine at least $5$ distinct distances must have $\gg n^2$ total distinct
    26в†’distances. This was disproved by Tao (2024).
    27в†’
    28в†’[Er97b] ErdЕ‘s, P., _Some of my favourite problems which recently have been solved_,
    29в†’Proceedings of the International Conference on Discrete Mathematics (ICDM) (1997).
    30в†’
    31в†’[Er97e] ErdЕ‘s, P., _Some old and new problems in combinatorial geometry_ (1997).
    32в†’
    33в†’[Ta24c] Tao, T., _A counterexample to the ErdЕ‘sвЂ“GyГЎrfГЎs conjecture on distances_ (2024).
    34в†’-/
    35в†’
    36в†’namespace Erdos135
    37в†’
    38в†’/-- The number of distinct pairwise distances in a finite point set $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$. -/
    39в†’noncomputable def numDistances (A : Finset (EuclideanSpace в„ќ (Fin 2))) : в„• :=
    40в†’  (((A Г—Лў A).filter (fun p => p.1 в‰  p.2)).image (fun p => dist p.1 p.2)).card
    41в†’
    42в†’/-- A finite point set $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ satisfies the "four-point, five-distance" property
    43в†’if every $4$-element subset determines at least $5$ distinct pairwise distances. -/
    44в†’def FourPointFiveDist (A : Finset (EuclideanSpace в„ќ (Fin 2))) : Prop :=
    45в†’  в€Ђ S : Finset (EuclideanSpace в„ќ (Fin 2)),
    46в†’    S вЉ† A в†’ S.card = 4 в†’ 5 в‰¤ numDistances S
    47в†’
    48в†’/--
    49в†’ErdЕ‘sвЂ“GyГЎrfГЎs Conjecture (Problem 135) [Er97b, Er97e]:
    50в†’Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ be a set of $n$ points such that any $4$ points determine at least
    51в†’$5$ distinct distances. Must $A$ determine $\gg n^2$ many distances?
    52в†’
    53в†’The conjecture asserts YES: there exists an absolute constant $c > 0$ such that
    54в†’every finite $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ with the four-point-five-distance property satisfies
    55в†’$|\text{distinct distances of } A| \geq c \cdot n^2$.
    56в†’
    57в†’This was answered in the NEGATIVE by Tao [Ta24c], who constructed for any
    58в†’large $n$ a set of $n$ points where any $4$ points determine at least $5$ distinct
    59в†’distances, yet the number of distinct distances is $O(n^2 / \sqrt{\log n})$.
    60в†’-/
    61в†’@[category research solved, AMS 5 52]
    62в†’theorem erdos_135 :
    63в†’    answer(False) в†”
    64в†’      в€ѓ c : в„ќ, 0 < c в€§
    65в†’        в€Ђ A : Finset (EuclideanSpace в„ќ (Fin 2)),
    66в†’          FourPointFiveDist A в†’
    67в†’          c * (A.card : в„ќ) ^ 2 в‰¤ (numDistances A : в„ќ) := by
    68в†’  sorry
    69в†’
    70в†’end Erdos135
    71в†’

import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports

/-!
# ErdЕ‘s Problem 135

*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/135](https://www.erdosproblems.com/135)

ErdЕ‘s and GyГЎrfГЎs conjectured that any set of $n$ points in the plane where every
$4$ points determine at least $5$ distinct distances must have $\gg n^2$ total distinct
distances. This was disproved by Tao (2024).

[Er97b] ErdЕ‘s, P., _Some of my favourite problems which recently have been solved_,
Proceedings of the International Conference on Discrete Mathematics (ICDM) (1997).

[Er97e] ErdЕ‘s, P., _Some old and new problems in combinatorial geometry_ (1997).

[Ta24c] Tao, T., _A counterexample to the ErdЕ‘sвЂ“GyГЎrfГЎs conjecture on distances_ (2024).
-/

namespace Erdos135

/-- The number of distinct pairwise distances in a finite point set $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$. -/
noncomputable def numDistances (A : Finset (EuclideanSpace в„ќ (Fin 2))) : в„• :=
  (((A Г—Лў A).filter (fun p => p.1 в‰  p.2)).image (fun p => dist p.1 p.2)).card

/-- A finite point set $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ satisfies the "four-point, five-distance" property
if every $4$-element subset determines at least $5$ distinct pairwise distances. -/
def FourPointFiveDist (A : Finset (EuclideanSpace в„ќ (Fin 2))) : Prop :=
  в€Ђ S : Finset (EuclideanSpace в„ќ (Fin 2)),
    S вЉ† A в†’ S.card = 4 в†’ 5 в‰¤ numDistances S

/--
ErdЕ‘sвЂ“GyГЎrfГЎs Conjecture (Problem 135) [Er97b, Er97e]:
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ be a set of $n$ points such that any $4$ points determine at least
$5$ distinct distances. Must $A$ determine $\gg n^2$ many distances?

The conjecture asserts YES: there exists an absolute constant $c > 0$ such that
every finite $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ with the four-point-five-distance property satisfies
$|\text{distinct distances of } A| \geq c \cdot n^2$.

This was answered in the NEGATIVE by Tao [Ta24c], who constructed for any
large $n$ a set of $n$ points where any $4$ points determine at least $5$ distinct
distances, yet the number of distinct distances is $O(n^2 / \sqrt{\log n})$.
-/
@[category research solved, AMS 5 52]
theorem erdos_135 :
    answer(False) в†”
      в€ѓ c : в„ќ, 0 < c в€§
        в€Ђ A : Finset (EuclideanSpace в„ќ (Fin 2)),
          FourPointFiveDist A в†’
          c * (A.card : в„ќ) ^ 2 в‰¤ (numDistances A : в„ќ) := by
  sorry

end Erdos135

   187в†’    simp [Matrix.det_fin_two, Matrix.det_fin_three, Module.Basis.toMatrix, this]
   188в†’  ring

   191в†’Given a finite set of points in the plane, we define the number of distinct distances between pairs
   192в†’of points.
   193в†’-/
   194в†’noncomputable def distinctDistances (points : Finset в„ќВІ) : в„• :=
   195в†’  (points.offDiag.image fun (pair : в„ќВІ Г— в„ќВІ) => dist pair.1 pair.2).card
   196в†’
   197в†’end EuclideanGeometry
   198в†’
   199в†’
   200в†’def IsIsosceles {О± : Type*} [Dist О±] (p q r : О±) : Prop :=
   201в†’  dist p q = dist q r в€Ё dist q r = dist r p в€Ё dist r p = dist p q
   202в†’
   203в†’nonrec def Set.IsIsosceles {О± : Type} [Dist О±] (A : Set О±) :=
   204в†’  Nonempty A в€§ A.Triplewise (IsIsosceles В· В· В·)
   205в†’

file.content:
suffices (a 0 - c 0) * (b 1 - c 1) - (b 0 - c 0) * (a 1 - c 1) =
      a 0 * b 1 - a 0 * c 1 - b 0 * a 1 + b 0 * c 1 + c 0 * a 1 - c 0 * b 1 by
    simp [Matrix.det_fin_two, Matrix.det_fin_three, Module.Basis.toMatrix, this]
  ring

/--
Given a finite set of points in the plane, we define the number of distinct distances between pairs
of points.
-/
noncomputable def distinctDistances (points : Finset в„ќВІ) : в„• :=
  (points.offDiag.image fun (pair : в„ќВІ Г— в„ќВІ) => dist pair.1 pair.2).card

end EuclideanGeometry


def IsIsosceles {О± : Type*} [Dist О±] (p q r : О±) : Prop :=
  dist p q = dist q r в€Ё dist q r = dist r p в€Ё dist r p = dist p q

nonrec def Set.IsIsosceles {О± : Type} [Dist О±] (A : Set О±) :=
  Nonempty A в€§ A.Triplewise (IsIsosceles В· В· В·)

1в†’/-
     2в†’Copyright 2025 The Formal Conjectures Authors.
     3в†’
     4в†’Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
     5в†’you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
     6в†’You may obtain a copy of the License at
     7в†’
     8в†’    https://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
     9в†’
    10в†’Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
    11в†’distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
    12в†’WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
    13в†’See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
    14в†’limitations under the License.
    15в†’-/
    16в†’
    17в†’import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18в†’
    19в†’/-!
    20в†’# ErdЕ‘s Problem 503
    21в†’
    22в†’*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/503](https://www.erdosproblems.com/503)
    23в†’-/
    24в†’
    25в†’namespace Erdos503
    26в†’
    27в†’open scoped EuclideanGeometry
    28в†’
    29в†’/--
    30в†’What is the size of the largest $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ such that every three points from $A$
    31в†’determine an isosceles triangle? That is, for any three points $x$, $y$, $z$ from $A$, at least two
    32в†’of the distances $|x - y|$, $|y - z|$, $|x - z|$ are equal.
    33в†’-/
    34в†’@[category research open, AMS 51]
    35в†’theorem erdos_503 (n : в„•) :
    36в†’    IsGreatest {(A.ncard) | (A : Set (в„ќ^n)) (hA : A.IsIsosceles)} answer(sorry) := by
    37в†’  sorry
    38в†’
    39в†’/--
    40в†’When $n = 2$, the answer is 6 (due to Kelly [ErKe47] - an alternative proof is given by KovГЎcs [Ko24c]).
    41в†’
    42в†’[ErKe47] ErdЕ‘s, Paul and Kelly, L. M., Elementary Problems and Solutions: Solutions: E735. Amer. Math. Monthly (1947), 227-229.
    43в†’[Ko24c] Z. KovГЎcs, A note on ErdЕ‘s's mysterious remark. arXiv:2412.05190 (2024).
    44в†’-/
    45в†’@[category research solved, AMS 51]
    46в†’theorem erdos_503.variants.R2 :
    47в†’    IsGreatest {(A.ncard) | (A : Set в„ќВІ) (hA : A.IsIsosceles)} 6 := by
    48в†’  sorry
    49в†’
    50в†’/--
    51в†’When $n = 3$, the answer is 8 (due to Croft [Cr62]).
    52в†’
    53в†’[Cr62] Croft, H. T., $9$-point and $7$-point configurations in $3$-space. Proc. London Math. Soc. (3) (1962), 400-424.
    54в†’-/
    55в†’@[category research solved, AMS 51]
    56в†’theorem erdos_503.variants.R3 :
    57в†’    IsGreatest {(A.ncard) | (A : Set в„ќВі) (hA : A.IsIsosceles)} 8 := by
    58в†’  sorry
    59в†’
    60в†’/--
    61в†’The best upper bound known in general is due to Blokhius [Bl84] who showed that
    62в†’$$
    63в†’|A| \leq \binom{n + 2}{2}
    64в†’$$
    65в†’
    66в†’[Bl84] Blokhuis, A., Few-distance sets. (1984), iv+70.
    67в†’-/
    68в†’@[category research solved, AMS 51]
    69в†’theorem erdos_503.variants.upper_bound (n : в„•) :
    70в†’    в€Ђ m в€€ {(A.ncard) | (A : Set (в„ќ^n)) (hA : A.IsIsosceles)}, m в‰¤ (n + 2).choose 2 := by
    71в†’  sorry
    72в†’
    73в†’/--
    74в†’Alweiss has observed a lower bound of $\binom{n + 1}{2}$ follows from considering the subset of
    75в†’$\mathbb{R}^{n + 1}$ formed of all vectors $e_i + e_j$ where $e_i$, $e_j$ are distinct coordinate
    76в†’vectors. This set can be viewed as a subset of some $\mathbb{R}^n$, and is easily checked to have
    77в†’the required property.
    78в†’-/
    79в†’@[category research solved, AMS 51]
    80в†’theorem erdos_503.variants.lower_bound (n : в„•) :
    81в†’    (n + 1).choose 2 в‰¤ sSup {(A.ncard) | (A : Set (в„ќ^n)) (hA : A.IsIsosceles)} := by
    82в†’  sorry
    83в†’
    84в†’end Erdos503
    85в†’

import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports

/-!
# ErdЕ‘s Problem 503

*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/503](https://www.erdosproblems.com/503)
-/

namespace Erdos503

open scoped EuclideanGeometry

/--
What is the size of the largest $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ such that every three points from $A$
determine an isosceles triangle? That is, for any three points $x$, $y$, $z$ from $A$, at least two
of the distances $|x - y|$, $|y - z|$, $|x - z|$ are equal.
-/
@[category research open, AMS 51]
theorem erdos_503 (n : в„•) :
    IsGreatest {(A.ncard) | (A : Set (в„ќ^n)) (hA : A.IsIsosceles)} answer(sorry) := by
  sorry

/--
When $n = 2$, the answer is 6 (due to Kelly [ErKe47] - an alternative proof is given by KovГЎcs [Ko24c]).

[ErKe47] ErdЕ‘s, Paul and Kelly, L. M., Elementary Problems and Solutions: Solutions: E735. Amer. Math. Monthly (1947), 227-229.
[Ko24c] Z. KovГЎcs, A note on ErdЕ‘s's mysterious remark. arXiv:2412.05190 (2024).
-/
@[category research solved, AMS 51]
theorem erdos_503.variants.R2 :
    IsGreatest {(A.ncard) | (A : Set в„ќВІ) (hA : A.IsIsosceles)} 6 := by
  sorry

/--
When $n = 3$, the answer is 8 (due to Croft [Cr62]).

[Cr62] Croft, H. T., $9$-point and $7$-point configurations in $3$-space. Proc. London Math. Soc. (3) (1962), 400-424.
-/
@[category research solved, AMS 51]
theorem erdos_503.variants.R3 :
    IsGreatest {(A.ncard) | (A : Set в„ќВі) (hA : A.IsIsosceles)} 8 := by
  sorry

/--
The best upper bound known in general is due to Blokhius [Bl84] who showed that
$$
|A| \leq \binom{n + 2}{2}
$$

[Bl84] Blokhuis, A., Few-distance sets. (1984), iv+70.
-/
@[category research solved, AMS 51]
theorem erdos_503.variants.upper_bound (n : в„•) :
    в€Ђ m в€€ {(A.ncard) | (A : Set (в„ќ^n)) (hA : A.IsIsosceles)}, m в‰¤ (n + 2).choose 2 := by
  sorry

/--
Alweiss has observed a lower bound of $\binom{n + 1}{2}$ follows from considering the subset of
$\mathbb{R}^{n + 1}$ formed of all vectors $e_i + e_j$ where $e_i$, $e_j$ are distinct coordinate
vectors. This set can be viewed as a subset of some $\mathbb{R}^n$, and is easily checked to have
the required property.
-/
@[category research solved, AMS 51]
theorem erdos_503.variants.lower_bound (n : в„•) :
    (n + 1).choose 2 в‰¤ sSup {(A.ncard) | (A : Set (в„ќ^n)) (hA : A.IsIsosceles)} := by
  sorry

end Erdos503

Now I have enough information to write a thorough review. Let me analyze all the aspects.

Key observations:

1. **Code reuse**: `numDistances` is duplicated identically in 135.lean and 657.lean. Also `distinctDistances` exists in FormalConjecturesForMathlib/Geometry/2d.lean using `offDiag` instead. The `NoIsoscelesTriangles` definition could potentially use `IsIsosceles` from 2d.lean but takes a different approach.

2. **Citations**: The website lists [Er73], and mentions Dumitrescu [Du08], Hunter/Alfaiz/Tang, and Straus. The formalization lists [Er73], [Er75f], [ErPa90], [Er97e]. Need to check if these match what's on the website.
   
3. **Variants and scope**: The website mentions extensions to R^k and references problem #135, but no higher-dimensional variants are formalized yet.

4. **Code quality**: The implementation is reasonably clean and readable.

5. **Problem precision**: The statement is well-defined enough for formalization.

6. **Definition verification**: I need to carefully check the `NoIsoscelesTriangles` definitionвЂ”for a 3-point set, the distance count should correctly identify when all three pairwise distances are distinct, which would rule out isosceles triangles.

The theorem's equivalence holds: requiring CВ·n distinct distances for any C > 0 is the same as requiring f(n)В·n distances where f(n) grows unbounded. Now I'm checking whether the formalization actually captures the original problem's requirement of f(n)В·n distinct distances for some unbounded f(n). for every C > 0, there exists Nв‚Ђ such that for all A with |A| в‰Ґ Nв‚Ђ and no isosceles triangles, numDistances(A) в‰Ґ CВ·|A|". 

This is indeed equivalent: if f(n) в†’ в€ћ, then for any C, eventually f(n) > C, so numDistances в‰Ґ f(n)В·n > CВ·n. Conversely, if the formalized version holds, define f(n) = min over all valid A of size n of numDistances(A)/n, then f(n) в†’ в€ћ.

Actually, there's a subtlety. The formalized statement quantifies over ALL sets A of size в‰Ґ Nв‚Ђ, not just sets of exactly size n. But this is fine - the equivalence still holds.

Now I'm thinking about what `NoIsoscelesTriangles` actually requires: for every 3-element subset, numDistances must equal 3, meaning all three pairwise distances are distinct. This rules out equilateral triangles and isosceles triangles, but I need to be careful about edge casesвЂ”in a Finset, elements are distinct points, so a 3-element subset genuinely has three distinct points in the Euclidean plane. I'm verifying that the formalization handles edge cases correctlyвЂ”collinear points still satisfy the distance condition even though they don't form a proper triangle, which aligns with the problem's focus on distinct distances rather than geometric position. The `numDistances S = 3` approach is mathematically sound for expressing "no isosceles triangles," though I should consider whether there's a more direct way to leverage existing definitions from the library.

content.text: Now I have all the information needed. Let me write the review.

input.content:
# Review: ErdЕ‘s Problem 657

## 1. Code Reuse

**`numDistances` is duplicated.** The identical definition appears in both `657.lean:37-38` and `135.lean:39-40`:

```lean
noncomputable def numDistances (A : Finset (EuclideanSpace в„ќ (Fin 2))) : в„• :=
  (((A Г—Лў A).filter (fun p => p.1 в‰  p.2)).image (fun p => dist p.1 p.2)).card
```

Additionally, `FormalConjecturesForMathlib/Geometry/2d.lean:194-195` provides `EuclideanGeometry.distinctDistances` using `offDiag` instead of `filter`:

```lean
noncomputable def distinctDistances (points : Finset в„ќВІ) : в„• :=
  (points.offDiag.image fun (pair : в„ќВІ Г— в„ќВІ) => dist pair.1 pair.2).card
```

The `offDiag` variant is cleaner and semantically equivalent. However, note the type difference: `Finset в„ќВІ` vs `Finset (EuclideanSpace в„ќ (Fin 2))`. These are definitionally equal but may cause friction in practice. A shared utility definition (or consolidation to the Mathlib variant) would reduce duplication across problems 93, 95, 135, 657, 958, and 959.

**`NoIsoscelesTriangles` could use `IsIsosceles`.** The file `FormalConjecturesForMathlib/Geometry/2d.lean:200-201` defines:

```lean
def IsIsosceles {О± : Type*} [Dist О±] (p q r : О±) : Prop :=
  dist p q = dist q r в€Ё dist q r = dist r p в€Ё dist r p = dist p q
```

The current `NoIsoscelesTriangles` definition (requiring `numDistances S = 3` for every 3-element subset) is mathematically equivalent to requiring that no triple of points satisfies `IsIsosceles`. Using `IsIsosceles` directly would avoid re-invoking the `numDistances` machinery for 3-element subsets. However, the current formulation has the advantage of being self-contained and directly mirroring the problem statement's language ("determines 3 distinct distances").

**Recommendation:** At minimum, deduplicate `numDistances` with Problem 135. Consider whether a shared definition in a utility file would be appropriate given the large number of ErdЕ‘s distance problems.

## 2. Citations

The formalization lists: `[Er73]`, `[Er75f]`, `[ErPa90]`, `[Er97e]`.

The website (erdosproblems.com/657) attributes the problem to ErdЕ‘s and Davies via [Er73], and mentions subsequent work by FГјredi, Ruzsa, and Pach. The website also references key results by:
- **Dumitrescu [Du08]**: established $(\\log n)^c \leq f(n) \leq 2^{O(\sqrt{\log n})}$
- **Hunter, Alfaiz, Tang**: improved lower bound to $2^{c(\log n)^{1/9}} \leq f(n)$
- **Straus**: observation about higher-dimensional constructions

The docstring references `[Er73]`, `[Er75f]`, `[ErPa90]`, `[Er97e]` without expanding the full citation details (journal, year, pages). Other files in the codebase (e.g., 135.lean, 503.lean) provide fuller citation information. The references should be expanded to include full titles at minimum, matching the convention used elsewhere. The progress results (Dumitrescu, Hunter et al.) are not mentioned in the docstring; while not strictly necessary, noting them would provide useful context.

## 3. Variants

The website notes:
- The problem extends to $\mathbb{R}^k$ for higher dimensions.
- **Straus's observation**: For $2^k \geq n$, there exist $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^k$ without isosceles triangles determining at most $n - 1$ distances (showing the answer may differ in higher dimensions).
- Related to Problem #135 (four-point-five-distance conjecture, which is the "next level" of the same type of question).

**No higher-dimensional variant is formalized.** The problem as stated is specifically for $\mathbb{R}^2$, which matches the core question. However, a natural generalization to $\mathbb{R}^k$ (parameterized by dimension) would capture the full scope of the problem. The cross-reference to Problem 135 is not mentioned in the docstring.

## 4. Readability

The code is clean and well-structured. Specific observations:

- The `numDistances` definition is clear but uses `A Г—Лў A` with a filter, which counts each ordered pair. Since `dist` is symmetric, the image step correctly collapses `(a, b)` and `(b, a)` to the same distance, so the result is correct despite using ordered pairs.
- The `NoIsoscelesTriangles` definition is readable. Quantifying over all 3-element subsets `S вЉ† A` is natural and mirrors the mathematical statement.
- The theorem statement's "equivalent reformulation" (в€Ђ C > 0, в€ѓ Nв‚Ђ, ...) is clearly documented in the docstring, which is helpful.
- The namespace `Erdos657` keeps definitions local, avoiding clashes with the duplicate `numDistances` in `Erdos135`.

**Minor suggestion:** The docstring could note that "no isosceles triangles" includes equilateral triangles (since equilateral is a special case of isosceles in standard mathematical convention). The formalization correctly excludes both, but this is worth noting for clarity.

## 5. Formalizability

**The problem is precisely formalizable.** The statement is unambiguous:

- "Set of $n$ points" в†’ `Finset (EuclideanSpace в„ќ (Fin 2))` with `A.card = n`.
- "Every 3-point subset determines 3 distinct distances" в†’ `NoIsoscelesTriangles` as defined.
- "At least $f(n) \cdot n$ distinct distances for some $f(n) \to \infty$" в†’ the equivalent в€Ђ C > 0, в€ѓ Nв‚Ђ formulation.

The only potential ambiguity is whether "no isosceles triangles" should exclude degenerate (collinear) configurations. In the current formalization, collinear triples are allowed as long as all three pairwise distances are distinct. This is the correct interpretation: the condition is about distances, not about whether the points form a non-degenerate triangle. A collinear triple with all distinct pairwise distances is not isosceles and should be permitted.

**Assessment: No ambiguity.** The problem is fully precise and the formalization captures it faithfully.

## 6. Correctness

**The formalization is mathematically correct.** Detailed verification:

1. **`numDistances` correctness:** For a finite set $A$, the definition computes $|\{d(p, q) : p, q \in A, p \neq q\}|$. Using ordered pairs and then taking the image under `dist` is correct because `dist` is symmetric, so `dist p q = dist q p`, and the image automatically deduplicates. The filter `p.1 в‰  p.2` correctly excludes self-distances (which are always 0).

2. **`NoIsoscelesTriangles` correctness:** For a 3-element subset $\{a, b, c\}$, `numDistances S = 3` means the three pairwise distances $d(a,b), d(a,c), d(b,c)$ are all distinct. This is exactly the condition that no isosceles triangle is formed (including equilateral as a special case). The quantification over all 3-element subsets correctly captures "no isosceles triangles in $A$."

3. **Equivalence of the reformulation:** The original asks for $f(n) \to \infty$ such that the number of distinct distances is $\geq f(n) \cdot n$. The formalized version states: $\forall C > 0, \exists N_0, \forall A$ with $|A| \geq N_0$ and no isosceles triangles, $\text{numDistances}(A) \geq C \cdot |A|$. This is a standard and correct reformulation of "$f(n) \to \infty$" in the sense that the minimum ratio $\text{numDistances}(A)/|A|$ over all valid $A$ of size $n$ tends to infinity. The equivalence is exact.

4. **Use of `answer(sorry)`:** The problem is open, and `answer(sorry)` correctly reflects that the truth value is unknown. The biconditional `answer(sorry) в†” [statement]` is the standard pattern for open problems in this codebase.

5. **Category tag:** `@[category research open, AMS 5 52]` correctly marks this as an open research problem in combinatorial geometry (AMS class 52).

**No mathematical errors detected.** The formalization is complete and correct for the core problem as stated on erdosproblems.com.

# Review: ErdЕ‘s Problem 657

## 1. Code Reuse

**`numDistances` is duplicated.** The identical definition appears in both `657.lean:37-38` and `135.lean:39-40`:

```lean
noncomputable def numDistances (A : Finset (EuclideanSpace в„ќ (Fin 2))) : в„• :=
  (((A Г—Лў A).filter (fun p => p.1 в‰  p.2)).image (fun p => dist p.1 p.2)).card
```

Additionally, `FormalConjecturesForMathlib/Geometry/2d.lean:194-195` provides `EuclideanGeometry.distinctDistances` using `offDiag` instead of `filter`:

```lean
noncomputable def distinctDistances (points : Finset в„ќВІ) : в„• :=
  (points.offDiag.image fun (pair : в„ќВІ Г— в„ќВІ) => dist pair.1 pair.2).card
```

The `offDiag` variant is cleaner and semantically equivalent. However, note the type difference: `Finset в„ќВІ` vs `Finset (EuclideanSpace в„ќ (Fin 2))`. These are definitionally equal but may cause friction in practice. A shared utility definition (or consolidation to the Mathlib variant) would reduce duplication across problems 93, 95, 135, 657, 958, and 959.

**`NoIsoscelesTriangles` could use `IsIsosceles`.** The file `FormalConjecturesForMathlib/Geometry/2d.lean:200-201` defines:

```lean
def IsIsosceles {О± : Type*} [Dist О±] (p q r : О±) : Prop :=
  dist p q = dist q r в€Ё dist q r = dist r p в€Ё dist r p = dist p q
```

The current `NoIsoscelesTriangles` definition (requiring `numDistances S = 3` for every 3-element subset) is mathematically equivalent to requiring that no triple of points satisfies `IsIsosceles`. Using `IsIsosceles` directly would avoid re-invoking the `numDistances` machinery for 3-element subsets. However, the current formulation has the advantage of being self-contained and directly mirroring the problem statement's language ("determines 3 distinct distances").

**Recommendation:** At minimum, deduplicate `numDistances` with Problem 135. Consider whether a shared definition in a utility file would be appropriate given the large number of ErdЕ‘s distance problems.

## 2. Citations

The formalization lists: `[Er73]`, `[Er75f]`, `[ErPa90]`, `[Er97e]`.

The website (erdosproblems.com/657) attributes the problem to ErdЕ‘s and Davies via [Er73], and mentions subsequent work by FГјredi, Ruzsa, and Pach. The website also references key results by:
- **Dumitrescu [Du08]**: established $(\\log n)^c \leq f(n) \leq 2^{O(\sqrt{\log n})}$
- **Hunter, Alfaiz, Tang**: improved lower bound to $2^{c(\log n)^{1/9}} \leq f(n)$
- **Straus**: observation about higher-dimensional constructions

The docstring references `[Er73]`, `[Er75f]`, `[ErPa90]`, `[Er97e]` without expanding the full citation details (journal, year, pages). Other files in the codebase (e.g., 135.lean, 503.lean) provide fuller citation information. The references should be expanded to include full titles at minimum, matching the convention used elsewhere. The progress results (Dumitrescu, Hunter et al.) are not mentioned in the docstring; while not strictly necessary, noting them would provide useful context.

## 3. Variants

The website notes:
- The problem extends to $\mathbb{R}^k$ for higher dimensions.
- **Straus's observation**: For $2^k \geq n$, there exist $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^k$ without isosceles triangles determining at most $n - 1$ distances (showing the answer may differ in higher dimensions).
- Related to Problem #135 (four-point-five-distance conjecture, which is the "next level" of the same type of question).

**No higher-dimensional variant is formalized.** The problem as stated is specifically for $\mathbb{R}^2$, which matches the core question. However, a natural generalization to $\mathbb{R}^k$ (parameterized by dimension) would capture the full scope of the problem. The cross-reference to Problem 135 is not mentioned in the docstring.

## 4. Readability

The code is clean and well-structured. Specific observations:

- The `numDistances` definition is clear but uses `A Г—Лў A` with a filter, which counts each ordered pair. Since `dist` is symmetric, the image step correctly collapses `(a, b)` and `(b, a)` to the same distance, so the result is correct despite using ordered pairs.
- The `NoIsoscelesTriangles` definition is readable. Quantifying over all 3-element subsets `S вЉ† A` is natural and mirrors the mathematical statement.
- The theorem statement's "equivalent reformulation" (в€Ђ C > 0, в€ѓ Nв‚Ђ, ...) is clearly documented in the docstring, which is helpful.
- The namespace `Erdos657` keeps definitions local, avoiding clashes with the duplicate `numDistances` in `Erdos135`.

**Minor suggestion:** The docstring could note that "no isosceles triangles" includes equilateral triangles (since equilateral is a special case of isosceles in standard mathematical convention). The formalization correctly excludes both, but this is worth noting for clarity.

## 5. Formalizability

**The problem is precisely formalizable.** The statement is unambiguous:

- "Set of $n$ points" в†’ `Finset (EuclideanSpace в„ќ (Fin 2))` with `A.card = n`.
- "Every 3-point subset determines 3 distinct distances" в†’ `NoIsoscelesTriangles` as defined.
- "At least $f(n) \cdot n$ distinct distances for some $f(n) \to \infty$" в†’ the equivalent в€Ђ C > 0, в€ѓ Nв‚Ђ formulation.

The only potential ambiguity is whether "no isosceles triangles" should exclude degenerate (collinear) configurations. In the current formalization, collinear triples are allowed as long as all three pairwise distances are distinct. This is the correct interpretation: the condition is about distances, not about whether the points form a non-degenerate triangle. A collinear triple with all distinct pairwise distances is not isosceles and should be permitted.

**Assessment: No ambiguity.** The problem is fully precise and the formalization captures it faithfully.

## 6. Correctness

**The formalization is mathematically correct.** Detailed verification:

1. **`numDistances` correctness:** For a finite set $A$, the definition computes $|\{d(p, q) : p, q \in A, p \neq q\}|$. Using ordered pairs and then taking the image under `dist` is correct because `dist` is symmetric, so `dist p q = dist q p`, and the image automatically deduplicates. The filter `p.1 в‰  p.2` correctly excludes self-distances (which are always 0).

2. **`NoIsoscelesTriangles` correctness:** For a 3-element subset $\{a, b, c\}$, `numDistances S = 3` means the three pairwise distances $d(a,b), d(a,c), d(b,c)$ are all distinct. This is exactly the condition that no isosceles triangle is formed (including equilateral as a special case). The quantification over all 3-element subsets correctly captures "no isosceles triangles in $A$."

3. **Equivalence of the reformulation:** The original asks for $f(n) \to \infty$ such that the number of distinct distances is $\geq f(n) \cdot n$. The formalized version states: $\forall C > 0, \exists N_0, \forall A$ with $|A| \geq N_0$ and no isosceles triangles, $\text{numDistances}(A) \geq C \cdot |A|$. This is a standard and correct reformulation of "$f(n) \to \infty$" in the sense that the minimum ratio $\text{numDistances}(A)/|A|$ over all valid $A$ of size $n$ tends to infinity. The equivalence is exact.

4. **Use of `answer(sorry)`:** The problem is open, and `answer(sorry)` correctly reflects that the truth value is unknown. The biconditional `answer(sorry) в†” [statement]` is the standard pattern for open problems in this codebase.

5. **Category tag:** `@[category research open, AMS 5 52]` correctly marks this as an open research problem in combinatorial geometry (AMS class 52).

**No mathematical errors detected.** The formalization is complete and correct for the core problem as stated on erdosproblems.com.

A shared `distinctDistances` also exists in `FormalConjecturesForMathlib/Geometry/2d.lean` using the cleaner `offDiag` approach. `IsIsosceles` from the same file could replace the `NoIsoscelesTriangles` definition but the current approach is reasonable.

5. **Formalizability**: Fully precise, no ambiguity. The в€ЂC>0 в€ѓNв‚Ђ reformulation is a standard and correct way to express $f(n) \to \infty$.

6. **Correctness**: Mathematically correct and complete. The `numDistances` definition handles symmetry correctly via image deduplication, `NoIsoscelesTriangles` correctly captures all-distinct pairwise distances, and the equivalence with the original $f(n) \to \infty$ formulation is exact.
